response: Significant bleeding with diarrhea is urgent. If ongoing or you feel weak/dizzy, go to the ER. We can arrange a provider call as well.
If you have significant bleeding or dark/tarry stools, you need urgent medical evaluation. If severe or ongoing, go to the ER and call our office.
If you suspect diverticulitis flare, contact us. Antibiotics may be needed and constipation prevention is important. If severe pain/fever, seek urgent care.
If this is life-threatening, hang up and dial 911 now.
If this is life-threatening, hang up and call 911.
If you have a significant reaction to H. pylori antibiotics, stop the meds and contact us. If severe symptoms (rash, breathing trouble, dehydration), go to urgent care/ER.
Fever after procedure or recurrent bloody stools should be escalated urgently. If severe or worsening, go to urgent care/ER and call our office.
If you can’t keep prep down and are dehydrated, go to the ER for fluids and call us immediately to coordinate whether to proceed or reschedule.
If H. pylori therapy is causing severe diarrhea, Dr. Shelby may advise stopping all antibiotics and using Imodium. Please contact the office promptly for follow-up.
If you went to the ER for dehydration during prep, call the office as soon as possible so we can coordinate whether to proceed or reschedule.
If you have a painful protruding growth, avoid trying to pop it. If pain is severe or worsening, seek urgent evaluation; we can schedule an in-person visit as soon as possible.
A painful protruding rectal mass could be a thrombosed hemorrhoid or other issue. If severe pain or worsening, seek urgent evaluation. We can also schedule the earliest in-person visit available.
Those are urgent symptoms—seek emergency care immediately (ER/911).
If dehydration is the main issue, ER fluids can stabilize you. After that, we’ll decide whether to proceed or reschedule—call us right away.
If this is a life-threatening emergency, hang up and call 911.
If the H. pylori antibiotics cause severe diarrhea or you feel very unwell, stop the medications and take Imodium as needed, then contact our office for follow-up.
If you have persistent diarrhea after antibiotics, C. diff should be ruled out promptly. Contact us—testing may be needed before proceeding with colonoscopy.
Vomiting blood or dark material can be serious. Seek urgent care/ER evaluation now.
If you can’t stop vomiting or feel dehydrated during prep, go to the ER for IV fluids. Call us as well—your procedure plan may need adjustment.
